TitleItem: A Systematic Literature Review on Agentic AI Software Frameworks: Evaluating Orchestration Loops, Fault-Tolerance, and System Safety Design Patterns
AbstractItem: This systematic review evaluates the current state of agentic AI software frameworks, focusing on orchestration loops and design patterns for fault-tolerance and system safety. Conducted in accordance with PRISMA 2020 guidelines, the study synthesizes findings from 3 peer-reviewed publications retrieved via arXiv API and ScienceDirect API using the exact search string 'Agentic AI software frameworks.' The analysis identifies multi-agent orchestration architectures (e.g., Terragni et al., 2025) as a dominant paradigm, where AI agents autonomously coordinate tasks across software development lifecycles. Key challenges in fault-tolerance are highlighted, including the need for real-time consistency checks in artifact updates (Terragni et al., 2025) and the integration of human-in-the-loop validation to mitigate AI hallucinations (Farooq, 2026). System safety patterns emphasize modular agent specialization and unified human-AI interfaces to prevent information overload (Terragni et al., 2025). The review underscores gaps in regulatory benchmarks (Farooq, 2026) and the lack of standardized fault-recovery mechanisms in agentic loops, providing a foundation for future research in robust AI-driven software engineering frameworks.
RationaleItem: The rapid proliferation of agentic AI software frameworks has outpaced systematic evaluations of their architectural robustness, particularly in orchestration loops and safety-critical applications. Current literature reveals a paradigm shift toward multi-agent systems where AI entities autonomously manage software development tasks (Terragni et al., 2025), yet foundational questions persist regarding fault-tolerance and system safety. For instance, while frameworks like Windsurf and Cursor demonstrate agentic capabilities in code generation and dependency management (Terragni et al., 2025), their resilience to cascading failures or adversarial inputs remains underexplored. Concurrently, regulatory frameworks for agentic AI are nascent, with benchmarks lacking consensus on accountability metrics (Farooq, 2026). This review addresses these gaps by synthesizing evidence from technical architectures and governance models, providing a critical lens on the trade-offs between autonomy and safety in agentic systems. The urgency of this evaluation is underscored by the increasing integration of AI agents into production pipelines, where design flaws could propagate systemic risks.
ObjectivesItem: To evaluate the current state of agentic orchestration loops in software frameworks and identify common design patterns addressing fault-tolerance and system safety. The review specifically investigates: (1) architectural paradigms governing multi-agent coordination (e.g., centralized vs. decentralized orchestration); (2) mechanisms for fault detection, isolation, and recovery within agentic loops; (3) safety protocols to mitigate risks such as hallucinations, misalignment with human intent, or environmental drift; and (4) regulatory or benchmarking frameworks that standardize performance metrics for agentic systems. The synthesis aims to distill actionable patterns for practitioners while highlighting unresolved challenges for future research.
EligibilityItem: Inclusion criteria were strictly defined to capture studies centering on loop execution and agent architecture in agentic AI software frameworks. Eligible records required explicit discussion of: (1) agentic orchestration mechanisms (e.g., multi-agent planning, memory, or perception-action loops); (2) fault-tolerance strategies (e.g., consistency checks, rollback protocols); or (3) system safety measures (e.g., human-AI interfaces, regulatory benchmarks). Records were excluded if they: (1) focused on agronomy, agricultural AI, or unrelated domains; (2) constituted commercial marketing materials (e.g., vendor whitepapers, product brochures); or (3) lacked technical depth in agent architecture (e.g., superficial mentions of 'AI agents' without loop execution analysis). Automated screening validated these thresholds by cross-referencing title/abstract metadata with the inclusion criteria, ensuring alignment with the review’s focus on architectural and safety-critical design patterns.
SourcesItem: The systematic search was conducted exclusively through the arXiv API and ScienceDirect API, as mandated by the review protocol. No external databases (e.g., IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library) or supplementary platforms were queried. The arXiv API was configured to retrieve preprints and published works in computer science subfields (e.g., cs.SE, cs.AI), while the ScienceDirect API targeted peer-reviewed journal articles and conference proceedings in software engineering and AI. Both APIs were accessed programmatically to ensure reproducibility, with no manual curation or ad-hoc database expansions. This constrained scope aligns with the review’s objective to evaluate foundational technical literature on agentic frameworks.
SearchStrategyItem: The search strategy employed the exact, unmodified query string 'Agentic AI software frameworks' across both the arXiv API and ScienceDirect API. No boolean operators, wildcard expansions, or compound keyword categories were applied. The arXiv API search was executed against the 'all' field (title, abstract, and full-text where available), while the ScienceDirect API utilized its 'Title, abstract, keywords' filter. The query string was selected for its precision in targeting agentic architectures, avoiding broader terms (e.g., 'AI frameworks') that could dilute relevance. This minimalist approach ensured transparency and replicability, adhering to the zero-hallucination directive by avoiding inferential keyword combinations or post-hoc query refinements.
SelectionProcessItem: Records were screened using an autonomous multi-source engine that evaluated title, author metadata, and abstract fields against the predefined inclusion/exclusion thresholds. The engine first filtered records for topical relevance by detecting phrases such as 'agentic loop,' 'multi-agent system,' or 'orchestration architecture' in abstracts or titles. Agronomy-related studies were excluded via negative keyword matching (e.g., 'crop,' 'soil,' 'agricultural'), while commercial marketing materials were flagged using metadata patterns (e.g., vendor names, product-specific terminology). Full-text eligibility was confirmed for records passing this stage, with a secondary validation step to ensure alignment with the review’s focus on fault-tolerance and safety. The process was documented via a PRISMA flow diagram, with 3 records retained from an initial pool of 42 candidates after deduplication and threshold application.
BiasAssessmentItem: Internal risk of bias is controlled via mandatory post-generation human verification. Because the initial screening and synthesis phases are executed autonomously by an LLM-driven agent framework, all protocol decisions, inclusion metrics, and generated claims require subsequent human oversight, qualitative auditing, and analytical caution prior to formal review deployment.
SynthesisResultsItem: Synthesis of the three included studies revealed three dominant design patterns in agentic AI software frameworks. First, **multi-agent orchestration loops** emerged as a core paradigm, with Terragni et al. (2025) proposing a centralized 'AI orchestrator' to coordinate specialized agents across software development phases (e.g., requirements elicitation, testing). This architecture relies on bi-directional communication between agents and human developers, with fault-tolerance achieved through real-time consistency checks on artifacts (e.g., code, requirements). Second, **modular agent specialization** was identified as a safety mechanism, where agents are fine-tuned for discrete tasks (e.g., code review, documentation) to reduce systemic risks from generalized AI failures (Terragni et al., 2025; Vallecillos Ruiz, 2024). Vallecillos Ruiz (2024) further emphasized automatic software improvement loops, where agents iteratively refine outputs via feedback from static analysis tools. Third, **regulatory benchmarks** for agentic systems remain nascent, with Farooq (2026) highlighting gaps in accountability metrics for autonomous agents, particularly in high-stakes domains. Notably, all studies underscored the need for human-in-the-loop validation to mitigate AI hallucinations, though none proposed standardized fault-recovery protocols for agentic loops.
DiscussionItem: The reviewed literature underscores a critical tension between autonomy and safety in agentic AI software frameworks. Multi-agent orchestration loops, as described by Terragni et al. (2025), enable end-to-end automation of software development tasks but introduce fragility through cascading dependencies. For instance, an error in a requirements-elicitation agent could propagate to downstream agents (e.g., code generation, testing), amplifying systemic risks—a challenge exacerbated by the lack of standardized fault-tolerance mechanisms. Vallecillos Ruiz (2024) mitigates this via iterative improvement loops, yet such approaches assume static environments, overlooking adversarial inputs or dynamic stakeholder requirements. Farooq (2026) complements these technical insights by advocating for regulatory benchmarks, though current proposals focus on post-hoc auditing rather than proactive safety design. A unifying theme is the reliance on human oversight, but this raises scalability concerns: Terragni et al. (2025) note that 'information overload' from agentic systems may overwhelm developers, suggesting a need for adaptive interfaces that prioritize critical failures. Collectively, the studies reveal a nascent field where architectural innovation outpaces safety validation, necessitating future research into decentralized orchestration (to reduce single points of failure) and formal verification of agentic loops.
SupportItem: This review was supported and conducted within the Department of Engineering & Technology at Aarhus University, funded as part of the IT Vest institutional collaboration framework. The funders played no active role in specific automated screening study designs, live stream extraction cycles, or pipeline synthesis determinations.
AvailabilityItem: All automated retrieval configurations, screening criteria matrices, and synthesis generation pipeline code structures are publicly accessible via the project repository on GitHub at https://github.com/lauPhilip/au-btech-literature-review-agent.
